summary: The post argues that the internet was originally decentralized but has been systematically centralized by capital investment, as corporations need central control to monetize their investments. Khailo coins 'Grass Computing' — running decentralized free software on your own hardware — as the antidote to cloud computing, and warns that decentralized systems are perpetually at risk of being co-opted.
key_points: The internet moved from decentralized to centralized, not the other way around
Capital investment favors centralized services because they enable control and monetization
Email, chat, and Git are all examples of inherently decentralized systems that became centralized
Convenience gaps between centralized and decentralized software are a funding problem, not a technical one
Basic income could fund more decentralized software development as a form of social capital investment
money_quotes: If Cloud Computing is running decentralized software on rented machines corralled behind a wall, Grass Computing is running decentralized free software on your own hardware in the open.
Their systems can be co-opted and centralized at any time.
Capitalists didn't understand what the internet was but knew it was important and started buying everything they could.
There is no technical reason why they could not do it. But there is no incentive for them to do it.
It is a constant fight not to have decentralized software become co-opted and centralized.
key_insight: The centralization of the internet is not a technical inevitability but a consequence of capital flowing toward controllable, monetizable architectures — and reversing it requires both engineering effort and social investment like basic income.
